questions.question: How many bits is an IPv6 address?
questions.explanation: IPv6 is a 128-bit Layer 3 address protocol — 4 times IPv4's 32 bits, yielding 2^128 addresses.

questions.question: How many hexadecimal quartets make up a full IPv6 address?
questions.explanation: An IPv6 address has 8 quartets (each 4 hex digits = 16 bits) separated by colons.

questions.question: What does the double colon (::) represent in an IPv6 address?
options: One run of consecutive all-0 quartets; may be used only once per address
Any number of consecutive non-zero quartets
A separator between network and host portions
questions.explanation: The double colon (::) replaces one run of consecutive all-0 quartets and may be used only once per address.

questions.question: What is the standard IPv6 prefix length for a subnet (the /64 convention)?
questions.explanation: The /64 convention is the standard IPv6 prefix length: 64 network bits + 64 host bits (the interface ID).

questions.question: What does EUI-64 do?
options: Builds a 64-bit interface ID from a 48-bit MAC by inserting FFFE and inverting the 7th bit
Generates a random 64-bit interface ID every boot
Encrypts the interface identifier for privacy
Compresses an IPv6 address to fit in 64 bits
questions.explanation: EUI-64 / Modified EUI-64 builds a 64-bit interface ID from a 48-bit MAC by splitting the MAC, inserting FFFE in the middle, and inverting the 7th bit.

questions.question: What does RIR stand for?
questions.explanation: RIR stands for Regional Internet Registry — the body that assigns IP address blocks (AFRINIC, APNIC, ARIN, LACNIC, RIPE NCC).

questions.question: Which command enables IPv6 routing on a Cisco router globally?
questions.explanation: `ipv6 unicast-routing` enables IPv6 routing on a Cisco router in global config mode.

questions.question: What does the `ipv6 enable` command do on an interface?
options: Enables IPv6 on the interface without assigning a global address (auto-creates link-local)
Assigns a configured global unicast address
questions.explanation: `ipv6 enable` turns on IPv6 on an interface without assigning a global address; it auto-creates a link-local address.

questions.question: What is the global routing prefix?
options: The leading /48 block an ISP assigns to an enterprise for a global unicast address
The last 64 bits of an IPv6 address
The 40-bit random portion of a unique local address
questions.explanation: The global routing prefix is the leading /48 block an ISP assigns to an enterprise for a global unicast address.

questions.question: Which command shows IPv6 addresses per interface (both global and link-local)?
questions.explanation: `show ipv6 interface brief` lists IPv6 addresses per interface, including both global and link-local.

questions.question: Which rule allows shortening an IPv6 address by dropping leading zeros?
options: 0s at the start of any quartet can be removed
0s at the end of any quartet can be removed
Entire quartets of 0s are always written as ::
questions.explanation: The leading 0s rule: 0s at the start of any quartet can be removed when shortening an IPv6 address.

questions.question: What does the interface identifier represent in an IPv6 address?
options: The first 48 bits (the global routing prefix)
questions.explanation: The interface identifier is the last 64 bits of an IPv6 address — the host portion.

questions.question: What does U/L bit refer to in EUI-64?
options: Universal/Local bit — 7th bit of a MAC, inverted during EUI-64
Upper/Lower bit — marks half of the MAC address
questions.explanation: U/L bit stands for Universal/Local bit — the 7th bit of a MAC address, inverted during the EUI-64 process.
